steps.title: Identify what comparison the conclusion requires
steps.content: The researchers concluded that longer leave times might not confer a greater cognitive benefit than shorter leave times. Supporting data must directly compare the two leave groups (2–4 days vs. 1–5 weeks) and show that the longer-leave group did not score higher than the shorter-leave group. Comparisons involving the no-leave group do not address this claim.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice against the figure and the conclusion
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘In the second test administration, participants who took 2–4 days of leave had higher average attentiveness scores than did those who took no leave, but in the third test administration, those who took no leave had higher average scores than those who took 1–5 weeks of leave.’: mixes in the no-leave group and does not consistently compare the two leave durations to each other across both tests. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘In the first test administration, participants who took 2–4 days of leave had lower average attentiveness scores than did those who took 1–5 weeks of leave and those who took no leave.’: the first test is the baseline, taken before leave was used, so it cannot speak to the cognitive effect of taking leave. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘In both the second and third test administrations, participants who took 2–4 days of leave had higher average attentiveness scores than did participants who took 1–5 weeks of leave.’: from the graph, the shorter-leave group's average attentiveness is at or above the longer-leave group's in both post-leave tests, which is exactly the pattern needed to support the conclusion that longer leave does not produce a greater benefit. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘In the second and third test administrations, participants who took 2–4 days of leave had higher average attentiveness scores than did those who took no leave.’: compares the shorter-leave group to the no-leave group, not to the longer-leave group, so it does not address whether longer leave outperforms shorter leave. ✗
